nativeRange.habitat: Shallow Caribbean coral reefs and rocky substrates, where it forms thin encrusting mats on hard surfaces; commonly found from reef flats down through reef slopes

decorPreferences: isolated frag rock or island rock (strongly recommended to prevent mat from spreading to main rockwork)
back wall or overflow box placement
vertical rock face or shaded ledge
sand-moat barrier around the dedicated colony rock

diet.notes: The Caribbean Encrusting Gorgonian derives most of its nutrition from symbiotic zooxanthellae (photosynthetic algae living within its tissues), and supplemental feeding is not required when the colony receives adequate light in an established reef system. Optional broadcast feeding with phytoplankton, rotifers, small particulate coral foods, or amino acid supplements has been reported to enhance growth and polyp extension.

goodWith: fast-growing soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: slow-growing SPS corals (Acropora, Montipora) — encrusting mat can overgrow and smother them
expensive LPS corals in proximity — at risk of encroachment
other gorgonians sharing the same rockwork — mat has been reported to encrust and slowly kill neighboring gorgonians
any coral placed on connected rockwork without a sand or glue barrier — the mat will spread across it

summary: The Caribbean Encrusting Gorgonian (Erythropodium caribaeorum) is a photosynthetic soft coral from the Caribbean that forms a thin, tan to brown mat covered with fine, hair-like polyps. It is generally considered one of the easier soft corals to keep, but tends to spread aggressively across any hard surface it can reach and is best isolated on a dedicated rock from the start.
careNotes:
RUNAWAY-SPREAD WARNING — much like the Indo-Pacific Green Star Polyp (Briareum violaceum), the Caribbean Encrusting Gorgonian is widely reported to encrust any hard surface it can reach: live rock, frag plugs, the bases of other corals, and even adjacent gorgonians, which it can slowly kill by overgrowing. It is generally advised to place the colony from the outset on an isolated rock or frag disc separated from the main rockwork by an open sand gap (5 cm or more is commonly recommended), on the back wall of the aquarium, or on an overflow box. Aquarists who introduce it directly onto main rockwork often report difficulty removing it later, because any fragment left behind can regenerate into a new colony. The species has been noted in the aquarium hobby press as one of the borderline 'pest' corals associated with historical Caribbean live-rock imports.

Distinction from GSP — the two corals look similar and are often confused, but they are not the same animal. Green Star Polyp in the hobby is Briareum violaceum, an Indo-Pacific species in the family Briareidae, recognizable by its vivid green polyps over a purple-red stoloniferous (sheet-like, spreading) mat. The Caribbean Encrusting Gorgonian is Erythropodium caribaeorum, in its own distinct family Erythropodiidae, native only to the Caribbean, the Bahamas, and South Florida. Its mat is tan to coffee-brown rather than green, the underside has a reddish tone from red internal sclerites (microscopic calcium carbonate structures), and the polyps are longer and finer — hair-like rather than starburst-shaped. Both species share the encrusting growth habit and tendency to spread aggressively, so the same containment approach applies to both, but they are separate genera.

Lighting and flow: generally considered very forgiving. The species is commonly described in the hobby as tolerant across a wide range of light levels, performing well from low to moderate light with a comfortable PAR window generally cited in the 50–200 range. Specimens that look pale or keep their polyps retracted under strong light are typically receiving too much rather than too little. Moderate to strong water movement is recommended to keep the long, hair-like polyp tentacles extended and to prevent detritus from settling on the mat. Direct, laminar flow tends to cause chronic polyp retraction; turbulent, indirect flow is generally preferred.

Water chemistry: standard reef parameters are sufficient — temperature 24–27°C, salinity SG 1.023–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm. As a soft coral, it has been reported as tolerant of slightly elevated nutrient levels and is considerably less demanding on calcium and alkalinity stability than stony corals (SPS).

Taxonomy note: Erythropodium caribaeorum was described by Duchassaing & Michelotti in 1860. Older literature sometimes places it in the family Anthothelidae; more recent revisions assign it to its own monotypic (single-genus) family Erythropodiidae within order Scleralcyonacea, the order that absorbed much of the older classification Alcyonacea. All sclerites are six-radiate, and the inner cortex sclerites are red — which gives the colony's underside its characteristic reddish color.

Fragging: the Caribbean Encrusting Gorgonian propagates readily by cutting a section of mat and gluing it onto a frag plug or piece of rubble. New frags typically extend polyps within a few days. Fragmentation is the primary mode of captive propagation; sexual reproduction by broadcast spawning has been documented in the wild but is not practiced in home aquaria.
breedingNotes: The Caribbean Encrusting Gorgonian reproduces in captivity vegetatively, through stolon expansion across available substrate and by simple fragmentation. A section of mat can be cut and attached to a new frag plug or piece of rubble with super glue gel or netting, then placed in moderate flow; polyps typically extend again within a few days. Sexual broadcast spawning has been documented in the wild but is not reported in home aquaria.

primary.name: The Reef Farm – Encrusting Gorgonian (Erythropodium caribaeorum)
primary.notes: Used for hobby-trade care profile: care level (described as 'bulletproof'), low light tolerance, moderate-to-high flow recommendation, optional supplemental feeding (TDO Chroma Boost A granules cited), placement guidance ('don't put this next to a rock you don't want it to grow on'), photosynthetic nature, and confirmation of the species' identity in the trade. World Wide Corals (the slice primary) returned only generic search results without a discrete species product page, so The Reef Farm was used as the substituted primary trade source.

additional.name: HeldeReef Aquatics – Encrusting Gorgonian
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temperature 24–26°C / 75–78°F, salinity 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm), lighting (moderate-to-high PAR 100–250 cited), flow (moderate-to-strong), and spread-management warning. Rated care level moderate (most other sources rate it easy/beginner); averaged toward the easier end given consistent 'hardy/bulletproof' descriptions elsewhere.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Erythropodium caribaeorum
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy and natural history: binomial Erythropodium caribaeorum (Duchassaing & Michelotti, 1860), order Scleralcyonacea, native range (Caribbean, Bahamas, South Florida), depth range 0.5–25 m, and biochemical note (produces eleutherobin).

additional.name: NSU Halmos College – Octocoral Guide: Erythropodium caribaeorum
additional.notes: Used for colony morphology (irregular encrusting mat <1 cm thick on hard substrate, hair-like tentacles when extended), sclerite detail (six-radiate; inner cortex red, outer cortex colorless), distinguishing feature from Briareum polyanthes (encrusts directly on hard surfaces rather than over other octocorals), and the Bahamas/South Florida/Caribbean range.

additional.name: AnimalAtlas – Encrusting Gorgonian (Erythropodium caribaeorum)
additional.notes: Cross-checked hobby-care framing: easy to keep and propagate, photosynthetic via zooxanthellae, aggressive overgrowth onto neighboring corals and rubble, color described as brown with purple underside from purple sclerites, polyps brown without stripes forming elongate hair-like tentacles in strong flow.

additional.name: Reef Builders – Top 5 Invasive Corals That Border on Being Aquarium Pests
additional.notes: Used as supporting context for the pest/invasive framing — flags Erythropodium caribaeorum as historically problematic when Caribbean live rock was widely imported, with recommendation to inspect rock and gorgonian bases for cryptic mat before introduction.
